Red Swastika School — P6 Science SA1 (Mid-Year Examination) 2022

Total Time: 1 hour 45 minutes  |  Booklet A: 56 marks (Q1–Q28, MCQ)  |  Booklet B: 44 marks (Q29–Q40, open-ended)

Booklet A — Multiple-Choice Questions [56 marks]

Question 1

2 marks

Study the chart below.

Which one of the following shows the correct heading for groups X and Y?

Group XGroup Y
(1)grows on landgrows in water
(2)has a weak stemhas a strong stem
(3)produces flowersdoes not produce flowers
(4)reproduces from sporesreproduces from seeds

Answer

Option 3. Group X (rose plant, mango tree) are flowering plants that reproduce by seeds, while Group Y (moss, bird's nest fern) are non-flowering plants that do not produce flowers. So X "produces flowers" and Y "does not produce flowers" is the correct heading.

Option 1 is wrong (all four plants grow on land). Option 2 is wrong (a mango tree has a strong woody stem). Option 4 reverses the groups — it is X that reproduces from seeds and Y from spores, not the other way round.

Question 2

2 marks

The table below shows some characteristics of the male reproductive cells in a flowering plant and a human.

Flowering plantHuman
Aproduced in the antherproduced in the testes
Brequired for pollination and fertilisationrequired for fertilisation
Cfuses with female reproductive cell in the stigmafuses with female reproductive cell in the ovary

Which of the following is/are correct?

  • C only
  • A and B only
  • B and C only
  • A, B and C

Answer

Option 2 (A and B only). A is correct: pollen (the male cell) is produced in the anther of a plant and sperm in the testes of a human. B is correct: the male cell is needed for fertilisation in both, and additionally for pollination in plants.

C is wrong: in a flowering plant the male and female reproductive cells fuse (fertilisation) in the ovule inside the ovary, not in the stigma. In a human the cells fuse in the fallopian tube (oviduct), not the ovary. So statement C is incorrect for both.

Question 3

2 marks

Some seedlings of the same height were planted in two identical pots, A and B, for two weeks. The pots had the same amount of soil and water. The pots were given the same amount of sunlight.

Which graph below shows the average height of the seedlings in Pots A and B?

Answer

Option 3. The seedlings in Pot A are spaced out (less crowded) so each plant gets more sunlight, water and space; they grow taller. The seedlings in Pot B are overcrowded, so they compete for resources and grow shorter. The correct graph must show Pot A taller than Pot B, with both still increasing over the days — graph (3) shows Pot A clearly above Pot B.

Graphs (1) and (4) place Pot B above Pot A (wrong order), and graph (2) also shows Pot B taller than Pot A, so they are incorrect.

Question 4

2 marks

Process X takes place in the human reproductive cell as shown below.

process X

Process X can also take place in plants. The diagram below shows the flowers of two plants. Both plants are of the same type.

Flowers undergo process Y before process X can take place in the flowers. Which arrow(s) show(s) process Y?

  • A only
  • A and B only
  • C and D only
  • B, C and D only

Answer

Option 4 (B, C and D only). Process X is fertilisation (sperm fusing with egg). Before fertilisation can happen in flowers, the flower must first be pollinated — process Y is pollination, the transfer of pollen grains from an anther to a stigma.

Arrows B, C and D land on a stigma (pollen reaching the stigma of a flower), so they show pollination. Arrow A points upward away from the flower (pollen leaving/blown off, not reaching a stigma), so it does not show pollination. Hence B, C and D only.

Question 5

2 marks

The diagram below shows how food and water are transported to and from different parts of a plant.

Which one of the following correctly shows the parts of the plant that are represented by A, B and C?

ABC
(1)rootsstemleaves
(2)stemleavesroots
(3)rootsleavesstem
(4)leavesrootsstem

Answer

Option 1. Water is absorbed by the roots (C, at the bottom) and carried up to the rest of the plant. Food is made in the leaves (A) by photosynthesis and transported to other parts. The stem (B) connects the roots and leaves and carries both water and food. Following the direction of the food and water arrows, A = leaves, B = stem, C = roots.

(Reading from the markings: the part that makes/sends out food is the leaves, the part that takes in water is the roots, and the linking part is the stem — giving roots, stem, leaves for A, B, C as in option 1.)

Question 6

2 marks

Muthu observed three cells, X, Y and Z, under the microscope. He recorded his observations in the table below. A tick (✓) indicates the presence of the part of the cell.

Part of cellCell XCell YCell Z
Cell wall✓✓
Nucleus✓✓✓
Chloroplasts✓

Muthu made some statements about the three cells.

A   Cell Y most likely belongs to an animal.
B   Cell X can be found in the stem of a plant.
C   Cell Z is most likely able to produce oxygen.

Which of the above statements is/are correct about cells X, Y and Z?

  • A only
  • C only
  • B and C only
  • A, B and C

Answer

Option 2 (C only). Cell Z has chloroplasts, so it can carry out photosynthesis and produce oxygen — statement C is correct.

A is wrong: Cell Y has a cell wall, which is a feature of plant cells, so Y is most likely a plant cell, not an animal cell. B is wrong: Cell X has no cell wall, so it is an animal cell and would not be found in the stem of a plant.

Question 7

2 marks

The diagram below shows how human systems work together.

Which systems do W and X represent and what are gases Y and Z?

System WSystem XGas YGas Z
(1)circulatoryrespiratoryoxygencarbon dioxide
(2)circulatoryrespiratorycarbon dioxideoxygen
(3)respiratorycirculatoryoxygencarbon dioxide
(4)respiratorycirculatorycarbon dioxideoxygen

Answer

Option 1. System W receives digested food from the digestive system and transports materials around the body — this is the circulatory system. System X exchanges gases with the surrounding air and exhaled air — this is the respiratory system.

Gas Y is taken in from the surrounding air into the body, so Y = oxygen. Gas Z is given out to the exhaled air, so Z = carbon dioxide. This matches option 1.

Question 8

2 marks

Which of the following organisms are decomposers?

A   grass
B   bacteria
C   caterpillar
D   mushrooms

  • A and C only
  • B and D only
  • A, B and D only
  • B, C and D only

Answer

Option 2 (B and D only). Decomposers break down dead organisms and waste matter. Bacteria (B) and mushrooms/fungi (D) are decomposers.

Grass (A) is a producer (it makes its own food). A caterpillar (C) is a consumer/plant-eater. Neither is a decomposer, so only B and D are correct.

Question 9

2 marks

The bar graph below shows the number of different organisms living in two different habitats, Q and R.

Based on the graph, which of the following statements is correct?

  • There are five populations in habitat R.
  • There are more populations in habitat Q than R.
  • There are nine communities in habitats Q and R.
  • There are twelve populations of woodlouse in habitat Q.

Answer

Option 2. A population is a group of organisms of the same kind living in the same habitat. Habitat Q has bars for more types of organisms than habitat R (habitat R is missing one or more organism types), so habitat Q has more populations than R.

Option 1 is wrong (habitat R does not have all five organisms present). Option 3 is wrong — all the organisms in one habitat form a single community, so the two habitats have two communities, not nine. Option 4 is wrong because a "population of woodlouse" is one population (twelve would be the number of woodlice, not the number of populations).

Question 10

2 marks

Study the food web below.

Which of the following statements about the food web are correct?

A   Y is a food producer.
B   U and X are plant-and-animal eaters.
C   T is the only plant-eater.
D   Population of Z will decrease when population of V decreases.

  • A and B only
  • B and C only
  • C and D only
  • A, B and D only

Answer

Option 3 (C and D only). In the food web the producer is the organism with no food arrows pointing into it. C is correct: T is the only organism that feeds directly on the producer, so it is the only plant-eater. D is correct: V is eaten by Z (arrow V→Z), so if the population of V decreases, Z has less food and the population of Z will decrease.

A is wrong: Y has food arrows pointing into it (it is eaten/feeds on others), so Y is not a producer. B is wrong: U and X feed only on the producer, so they are plant-eaters, not plant-and-animal eaters. Hence only C and D are correct.

Question 11

2 marks

The diagram below shows three organisms, A, B and C, which live in the same pond habitat.

James observed for an hour the movement of organisms A, B and C in three areas, X, Y and Z, of the pond.

He put a tick in the box below if the organisms entered an area.

OrganismArea XArea YArea Z
A✓
B✓✓
C✓✓✓

Based on the information above, which one of the following statements is correct?

  • The prey of organism A can only be found in Area Z.
  • Organism A is least adapted to look for food at the bottom of the pond.
  • Organisms B and C do not need oxygen as they are able to go below area X.
  • Only organism A will survive if a layer of oil was poured on the surface of the water.

Answer

Option 2. Organism A only ever enters Area X (the water surface) and never goes to the middle (Y) or bottom (Z). So among the three organisms, A is the least able (least adapted) to look for food at the bottom of the pond.

Option 1 is wrong: A stays only in Area X, so its prey must be at the surface, not at the bottom (Z). Option 3 is wrong: all living things, including B and C, need oxygen. Option 4 is wrong: organism A breathes air at the surface through its air tube, so a layer of oil on the surface would block it and harm A — the gilled fish C breathing dissolved oxygen below would be less affected.

Question 12

2 marks

P, Q, R and S are airtight jars which contained either water plants or water plants with water snails. Jars P and Q were placed in a garden while Jars R and S were placed in a black box in a room.

At the end of the experiment, which jar would have the least amount of carbon dioxide?

  • P
  • Q
  • R
  • S

Answer

Option 2 (Q). Jar Q is in the garden (light) and contains a water plant only (no water snail). In the light the plant photosynthesises and uses up carbon dioxide, and because there is no snail, no carbon dioxide is added by an animal's respiration. So jar Q ends up with the least carbon dioxide.

Jars R and S are in the dark (black box), so their plants cannot photosynthesise and only respire, adding carbon dioxide. Jar P is in the light but also contains a water snail, whose respiration adds carbon dioxide. Thus Q (light, plant only) has the least carbon dioxide.

Question 13

2 marks

Bala set up the apparatus below to investigate the factors affecting the rate of photosynthesis.

What can Bala do to make the ink move to the right faster?

A   Add more water plants in the test tube.
B   Move the lamp closer to the water plant.
C   Remove the water plant in the test tube.
D   Move the lamp further away from the water plant.

  • A and B only
  • A and D only
  • B and C only
  • C and D only

Answer

Option 1 (A and B only). The ink moves to the right as oxygen is produced by photosynthesis. To speed up photosynthesis (more oxygen, ink moves faster): A — adding more water plants gives more plant material to photosynthesise; B — moving the lamp closer gives more light. Both increase the rate of photosynthesis.

C is wrong: removing the plant stops photosynthesis altogether. D is wrong: moving the lamp further away reduces the light and slows photosynthesis, so the ink would move slower, not faster.

Question 14

2 marks

The diagram below shows a leaf of a plant used in an experiment. Chlorophyll is only present in the green part of the leaf. At the start of the experiment, there was no starch in the leaf. Next, the leaf was partly covered by black paper.

The plant was then put in the sun. After several hours, four discs, W, X, Y and Z, were punched out from the leaf in the positions shown above. The discs were tested for starch using iodine solution. Iodine solution turns from yellowish-brown to blue-black in the presence of starch.

Which one of the following shows the correct colour on the four discs?

Answer

Option 4. A disc only turns blue-black (starch present) if its part of the leaf had BOTH chlorophyll (green) AND light (not covered by black paper). A disc stays yellowish-brown if it lacked chlorophyll (white region) OR was covered by the black paper (no light).

Only the disc that was green and uncovered makes starch and turns blue-black; the white-region disc, the covered disc, and the disc that is both white/covered stay yellowish-brown. Option 4 is the only choice that correctly shows just the green-and-lit disc as blue-black and the rest as yellowish-brown.

Question 15

2 marks

Study the diagram below.

Based on the diagram above, which of the following correctly identifies the properties of material J?

waterproofflexiblegood conductor of heat
(1)noyesno
(2)yesyesnot possible to tell
(3)yesnono
(4)yesnonot possible to tell

Answer

Option 4. In the chart, material J is reached by following "not flexible" then "waterproof". So J is waterproof = yes and flexible = no. Because J is on the "not flexible" branch, the chart never tests its heat conductivity, so whether it is a good conductor of heat is not possible to tell from the diagram.

Options 1 and 2 wrongly say J is flexible. Option 3 wrongly states "no" for good conductor of heat when the chart gives no information about it. Option 4 matches: yes, no, not possible to tell.

Question 16

2 marks

A piece of ice was taken out from the freezer and left on a table at room temperature as shown in the diagram below.

Which of the following statements is correct?

  • The water gained heat from the ice cube.
  • The water lost heat to the surroundings.
  • The ice cube gained heat from the surrounding air.
  • The surrounding air gained heat from the ice cube.

Answer

Option 3. The ice cube is colder than the surrounding air. Heat always flows from a hotter object to a colder one, so the ice cube gains heat from the surrounding air, causing it to melt.

Options 1 and 4 are wrong because heat flows from the warmer surroundings/water to the colder ice, not the other way. Option 2 is wrong: the water formed is colder than the surroundings, so it gains heat from the surroundings rather than losing heat to them.

Question 17

2 marks

The statements below describe the properties of substance S at different temperatures.

  • At 30°C, S has definite volume and shape.
  • At 45°C, S does not have a definite shape but a definite volume.
  • At 95°C, S can be compressed.

Which of the following are the possible melting and boiling points of substance S described above?

melting point (°C)boiling point (°C)
(1)25110
(2)35100
(3)4080
(4)5075

Answer

Option 3. At 30°C S is a solid (definite volume and shape), so the melting point must be above 30°C. At 45°C S is a liquid (definite volume, no definite shape), so the melting point is below 45°C and the boiling point above 45°C. At 95°C S is a gas (can be compressed), so the boiling point is below 95°C.

So melting point is between 30°C and 45°C, and boiling point is between 45°C and 95°C. Only option 3 (melting 40°C, boiling 80°C) fits both ranges. Options 1 (mp 25) and 4 (mp 50) fail the melting range; option 2 (bp 100) fails the boiling range.

Question 18

2 marks

Dominic carried out an experiment to find out whether the presence of wind affects the rate of evaporation of water.

Which of the following should he use as a control set-up for the experiment?

Answer

Option 4. A control set-up must be identical to the experiment in every way except the one variable being tested — here, the presence of wind (the fan). The experiment set-up has a container with an opening and a fan; the control should keep the same container with the same opening but have no fan. Option 4 shows exactly this (same opening, pail of water, but no fan).

Options 1 and 3 still contain fans (so they do not remove the wind variable). Option 2 has no opening, so it changes a second variable as well and is not a fair control.

Question 19

2 marks

Which of the following statements show safe use of electricity?

A   Handling electrical appliances when hands are wet.
B   Checking electrical appliances for any exposed wires.
C   Plugging in many electrical appliances into one socket.
D   Getting an electrician to repair damaged electrical appliances.

  • A and B only
  • A and D only
  • B and C only
  • B and D only

Answer

Option 4 (B and D only). B is safe — checking for exposed wires prevents electric shocks. D is safe — a qualified electrician should repair damaged appliances.

A is dangerous: water conducts electricity, so handling appliances with wet hands risks an electric shock. C is dangerous: overloading one socket with many appliances can cause overheating and fire. So only B and D are safe.

Question 20

2 marks

Two circuits with objects A, B and C were set up as shown below.

Objects A, B and C were rearranged to form a new circuit below.

How many bulb(s) would light up in the new circuit?

  • 1
  • 5
  • 3
  • 4

Answer

Option 4 (4). From the first two circuits: the bulb did not light with A and B, but lit with A and C. This tells us A is a conductor and C is a conductor, while B is a non-conductor (it stops the current, so the bulb did not light when B was in the circuit).

In the new circuit, any branch or loop that has to pass through object B is broken (no current), while branches passing only through conductors A and C work. Tracing the circuit, the bulbs on the working (conducting) paths light up and the bulb(s) on the path blocked by B do not — giving 4 bulbs lit.

Question 21

2 marks

Four circuits, P, Q, R and S, were set up as shown below.

Which of the following arrangements shows the brightness of bulb X in the circuits correctly?

Dimmest→→Brightest
(1)PQSR
(2)PRQS
(3)RSQP
(4)SRQP

Answer

Option 1. Bulb X is brightest when it gets the most electric current. More batteries in series and fewer bulbs sharing the current make X brighter; more bulbs in series (sharing the battery) or fewer batteries make X dimmer. Comparing the four circuits, P (bulbs in series, fewest batteries per bulb) is the dimmest and R is the brightest, with Q and S in between.

Arranging from dimmest to brightest gives P, Q, S, R, which is option 1.

Question 22

2 marks

A ball is moving towards Roger as shown in the diagram below.

Which of the following change(s) would take place in the ball when Roger hit the ball with his racket?

A   Change in mass
B   Change in speed
C   Change in direction

  • A only
  • B only
  • B and C only
  • A, B and C

Answer

Option 3 (B and C only). When Roger hits the ball, the force from the racket changes the ball's speed (B) and its direction of motion (C) — the ball is sent back the other way, faster or slower.

A is wrong: a force does not change the amount of matter in the ball, so its mass stays the same. Only the speed and direction change.

Question 23

2 marks

Vanessa conducted an experiment using the set-up below.

Based on the results shown above, which distance should Vanessa measure to determine the extension of the spring caused by the wooden block?

  • W to Y
  • W to Z
  • X to Y
  • X to Z

Answer

Option 3 (X to Y). The extension is how much further the spring stretches because of the wooden block. X marks the bottom of the spring (or the load mark) before the block is added, and Y marks the same point after the block is added. The difference between these two positions (X to Y) is the extension caused by the block.

Distances measured from W (the fixed top of the spring) give the whole stretched length, not the extra stretch caused by the block, so options 1 and 2 are wrong. X to Z (option 4) goes past the relevant mark and does not give the block's extension.

Question 24

2 marks

Jaime conducted an experiment using the set-up below.

She moved the magnet towards the steel pin and recorded the distance d at which the steel pin is first attracted to the magnet. She repeated the experiment with three other magnets of different sizes and the results are shown below.

magnetdistance d (cm)
A10
B6
C2

Based on the results above, what could Jaime infer from her experiment?

A   Only magnet A can attract the pin from 7 cm away.
B   Magnet C has a greater magnetic strength than magnet B.
C   The bigger the size of the magnet, the greater the magnetic strength.

  • A only
  • A and B only
  • B and C only
  • A, B and C

Answer

Option 1 (A only). The larger the distance d, the stronger the magnet (it can attract the pin from further away). Magnet A attracts the pin from 10 cm, so only magnet A can attract the pin from 7 cm away (B reaches only 6 cm, C only 2 cm). Statement A is correct.

B is wrong: magnet C attracts from only 2 cm while B attracts from 6 cm, so C is weaker than B, not stronger. C is wrong: the question does not tell us the order of the magnets' sizes, so we cannot conclude that a bigger magnet is always stronger from these results.

Question 25

2 marks

Ahmad pasted magnet M on an electronic balance as shown in diagram 1.

Diagram 1

When another magnet that was clamped on a retort stand was brought near to magnet M, Ahmad noticed that the readings on the electronic balance changed as shown in diagrams 2 and 3.

Diagram 2     Diagram 3

Which of the following shows the likely readings on the electronic balance for X and Y?

XY
(1)6090
(2)7060
(3)8080
(4)9060

Answer

Option 2 (X = 70, Y = 60). In Diagram 1 magnet M sits alone on the balance, so the balance shows M's own weight: X = 70 units. In Diagram 2 the magnet above has a like pole facing M's pole, so the two magnets repel; the upper magnet pushes M down onto the balance, so the reading is more than M’s weight (80 units). In Diagram 3 the magnet above is turned so that unlike poles face each other, so the two magnets attract; the upper magnet pulls M upwards, so the balance reads less than its weight: Y = 60 units. M’s true weight (70) lies between the two readings, so X = 70 and Y = 60 — option 2.

Question 26

2 marks

The diagram below shows how a shadow of an object can be cast on a screen using a torch. Only the torch and the screen can be moved.

Which of the following correctly shows how the height of the shadow will be affected when distance x and y is/are changed?

distance xdistance yheight of shadow
(1)decreaseremains the sameincrease
(2)increaseremains the sameincrease
(3)remains the sameincreaseremains the same
(4)remains the samedecreaseincrease

Answer

Option 1. Distance x is from the torch to the object; distance y is from the object to the screen. If x decreases (torch moved closer to the object) while y stays the same, the light spreads out more after passing the object, so the shadow on the screen becomes larger (height increases).

Option 2 is wrong (moving the torch further away, increasing x, makes the shadow smaller). Option 3 is wrong (increasing y makes the shadow bigger, not the same). Option 4 is wrong (decreasing y makes the shadow smaller, not larger).

Question 27

2 marks

Nurul threw a pebble from point A and hit the box off the stand at point B. The box then fell off the stand from point B to point C before hitting the ground.

Which of the following correctly shows the change in the amount of energy possessed by the pebble and the box?

change in potential energy of pebble from A to Bchange in kinetic energy of box from point B to C
(1)decreasedecrease
(2)decreaseincrease
(3)increasedecrease
(4)increaseincrease

Answer

Option 4 (increase, increase). The pebble travels from A up to B (it rises to a higher position), so its height increases and its gravitational potential energy increases from A to B.

The box falls from B down to C, moving faster as it falls, so its speed increases and its kinetic energy increases from B to C. Both energies increase, which is option 4.

Question 28

2 marks

Shannon had four mugs of the same thickness and size but they were made of different materials, W, X, Y and Z. She filled each mug with 250 ml of hot water at 80°C. The temperature of the water in the mug was measured every two minutes. The results of the experiment were plotted in the graph below.

Which of the following statement(s) about the four materials is/are correct?

A   Material Z is the best conductor of heat.
B   Material Y is a poorer conductor of heat than material X.
C   Material W is a better conductor of heat than material X.

  • A only
  • B only
  • A and C only
  • B and C only

Answer

Option 1 (A only). The faster the water cools, the better that mug's material conducts heat away. On the graph, the water in mug Z cools the fastest (its line drops the most), so material Z is the best conductor of heat — statement A is correct.

B is wrong: Y's water cools faster than X's, so Y is a better conductor than X, not poorer. C is wrong: W's water cools the slowest (stays hottest), so W is a poorer conductor than X, not better. Only A is correct.

Booklet B — Open-Ended Questions [44 marks]

Question 29

2 marks

Study the plant and human reproductive system as shown below.

plant reproductive system     human reproductive system

(a) State one similarity in the function of parts A and X. (1m)
(b) If only part X is removed, can fertilisation still take place in a human reproductive system? Explain your answer. (1m)

Answer

(a) Both part A (the ovary of the flower) and part X (an ovary in the human) produce the female reproductive cell — the ovule/egg in the plant and the egg in the human.
(b) Yes. If part X is removed, part Y (the ovary) can still produce the female reproductive cell (egg). The male reproductive cell (sperm) can still fuse with the female reproductive cell, so fertilisation can still take place.

Question 30

3 marks

Seth recorded his observations of fruits A, B and C in the table below.

Description of fruitfruit Afruit Bfruit C
Is edibleyesyesno
Has fibrous husknoyesno
Is juicy and fleshyyesnono
Has wing-like structurenonoyes
(a) What is the likely method of seed dispersal for fruit A? Explain your answer. (1m)

The diagram below shows the seed dispersal pattern of one of the fruits.

Key:   ◉ parent plant    ◎ young plant

(b) Which fruit (A, B or C) would likely be dispersed by the method shown in the diagram? Explain your answer. (2m)

Answer

(a) Fruit A is dispersed by animals. Fruit A is juicy and fleshy (and edible), which attracts animals to eat it; the seeds then pass out of the animal or are dropped away from the parent plant.
(b) Fruit B. The diagram shows the young plants spread out along the flow of the river, so the fruit is dispersed by water. Fruit B has a fibrous husk which contains air spaces; this allows fruit B to float on water and be carried along the river before landing on the river bank.

Question 31

4 marks

Stephen conducted an experiment as shown below. Each beaker contains 200 cm³ of water as shown below. The set-ups were placed next to a window.

After two days, the volume of water in each beaker was measured and recorded in the table below.

PlantVolume of water at the start of experiment (cm³)Volume of water left (cm³)
A200160
B200180
C200200
D200190
(a) Stephen's friend, Ali, pointed out that the volume of water left in beaker D was incorrect. Explain why this is so. (2m)
(b) Which two set-ups should be compared to show that the roots of the plant absorb water? Give a reason for your answer. (1m)
(c) How does putting a layer of oil ensure that the volume of water taken in by the plant is accurate? (1m)

Answer

(a) Beaker D should have less water left than beaker B, because the plant in beaker D has more leaves than the plant in beaker B. Since the plant in D loses more water through the tiny openings (stomata) of its many leaves by transpiration, more water is taken in by its roots, so the volume of water left should be lower than B's, not higher.
(b) Set-up A and set-up C. The aim is to find out whether the roots absorb water, so only the presence of roots should differ between the two set-ups while all other variables are kept the same. (Set-up C has no roots / cannot absorb water, so its volume stays at 200 cm³.)
(c) The layer of oil prevents water from evaporating from the surface of the beaker. This ensures the loss of water is only due to the water absorbed (taken in) by the roots of the plant and not due to evaporation, so the measurement is accurate.

Question 32

4 marks

Ming Fa conducted an experiment using three identical pieces of paper, X, Y and Z. Paper X was left unfolded while papers Y and Z were folded as shown in the diagram below.

Each piece of paper was dropped from the same height. The time taken for each piece to reach the ground was recorded in the table as shown below.

PaperXYZ
Time taken for paper to reach the ground (s)8——
(a) Based on the above result, what could Ming Fa conclude about the surface area of the paper and the time taken for it to reach the ground? (1m)

Study the features of two organisms, A and B, as shown below. They are of similar sizes.

(b) Organisms A and B jumped down from the same height of a tree. Using the findings from Ming Fa's experiment, explain why organism A took a longer time to reach the ground. (1m)
(c) Organism A lives in the forest. Suggest two advantages of having stretched loose skin that will help organism A to survive better. (2m)

Answer

(a) The greater the surface area of the paper, the longer the time taken for the paper to reach the ground. (Paper X, with the largest surface area, took the longest time.)
(b) Organism A has stretched loose skin which gives it a greater surface area than organism B. The larger surface area allows organism A to stay in the air for a longer time, so it takes a longer time to reach the ground.
(c) Any two advantages, e.g.:
(i) It allows the organism to stay longer in the air to escape from its predators.
(ii) It allows the organism to glide a further distance (between trees) to find its food/prey more easily.

Question 33

3 marks

The diagram below shows an incomplete food web in a community.

(a) Using the following information, complete the food web by filling in the blank circles with the letters X, Y and Z. (1m)
• X is a plant eater.
• Both Y and Z are food producers.
• E feeds on Z.
(b) Based on the food web, explain how overhunting of organism D leads to the change in the population of organism B. (2m)

Answer

(a) The completed food web is shown below. The top-middle blank circle is X (the plant eater, eaten by B), the centre-left blank circle is Y and the right blank circle is Z (both producers; E feeds on Z).
(b) When organism D is overhunted, there will be more food producer Y and Z left (less D to eat them), so there is more food for plant-eater X and the population of X increases. With more X for organism C and B to feed on, there is more food for B, so the population of organism B increases.
(Accept the alternative chain: with less D, organism C / A has less to feed on, and as A can only feed on B, the population of B decreases.)

Question 34

4 marks

Lily collected three samples of water from three different ponds, X, Y and Z. Using the set-up below, she placed each sample of water in front of the light sensor of the datalogger. She recorded the amount of light that passed through each sample of water.

Her results are shown below.

Sample of water from pondAmount of light measured by the sensor (units)
X1
Y143
Z266
(a) If a coin is dropped into the three beakers containing samples of water from pond X, Y and Z, in which beaker will the coin be most visible? Explain. (1m)

Both plants A and B are the same. Lily placed some plant A in water from pond X and plant B in water from pond Z. She observed that plant B grew better than plant A.

(b) Give a reason for her observation. (1m)

In another experiment, Lily measured the amount of oxygen present in the water at different temperatures. The results are shown in the graph below.

Lily had some fish in a tank of water. She noted that the breathing rate of the fish increased when the temperature of the water in the fish tank increased.

(c) Using the results of the experiment, explain her observation. (2m)

Answer

(a) The coin will be most visible in the beaker containing water from pond Z. The amount of light measured by the sensor for pond Z is the greatest (266 units), meaning this water allows the most light to pass through. Since more light passes through, the coin can be seen most clearly.
(b) Water from pond Z allows more light to pass through, so plant B received more light. With more light, plant B could photosynthesise at a faster rate to make more food and grow better than plant A (which was in the murky pond X water that let little light through).
(c) From the graph, as the temperature of the water increases, the amount of oxygen dissolved in the water decreases. So when the fish-tank water became warmer, there was less oxygen available in the water. The fish therefore had to breathe faster to take in enough oxygen to survive.

Question 35

4 marks
(a) State a difference between boiling and evaporation. (1m)

When Sophie took out a tray of ice cubes from the freezer and placed it on the table, she observed that there was 'white mist' appearing above the tray of ice cubes as shown in the diagram below.

(b) State whether the 'white mist' is a solid, liquid or gas and explain how the mist was formed. (2m)

After an hour, Sophie noticed that all the ice cubes had melted and some water was formed under the tray even though she did not spill any water from the tray.

(c) Explain how the water was formed. (1m)

Answer

(a) Boiling only takes place at a fixed temperature (the boiling point), while evaporation takes place at any temperature (below the boiling point).
(b) The 'white mist' is a liquid (tiny water droplets). The water vapour in the surrounding air came into contact with the cold air just above the ice cubes, lost heat to the cold surroundings and condensed into tiny water droplets, which we see as the white mist.
(c) The water vapour in the surrounding air came into contact with the cold outer surface of the tray, lost heat and condensed into water droplets. These droplets collected and formed the water under the tray (it did not come from inside the tray).

Question 36

5 marks

Mark wanted to investigate how the arrangements of bulbs in a circuit would affect the brightness of the bulbs. He used all the apparatus listed below to create set-ups A and B.

• four identical bulbs   • wires   • four identical batteries

After setting up his experiment, he realised that when one of the bulbs in set-up A fused, the other bulb in the set-up did not light up. This did not happen in set-up B.

(a) The diagram below shows parts of the circuit. Complete the circuit by drawing the wires to show the arrangement of the bulbs in the circuit in set-ups A and B. (2m)

Mark wanted to create a light system that would allow him to control the brightness of the lamp depending on which switch, A, B or C, was closed.

The circuit diagram below shows how switches A, B and C were connected to the lamp.

(b)(i) Mark noticed that the brightness of the lamp changed when he closed switch C as compared to when he closed switch A. Will the light of the lamp be brighter or dimmer? Explain why. (2m)
(b)(ii) Explain why the lamp did not light up when Mark closed switch B. (1m)

Answer

(a) Because in set-up A one bulb going out switches off the other, the two bulbs must be in series (one single loop). In set-up B the other bulb stays lit, so the two bulbs must be in parallel (each on its own branch). The completed circuits are shown below.
(b)(i) The lamp will be brighter. When switch C is closed, more batteries are connected in the circuit than when switch A is closed. More batteries provide more electricity (a greater current) flowing through the lamp, so it lights up more brightly.
(b)(ii) When switch B is closed, the battery in that branch is connected the wrong way round (terminals connected incorrectly), so the circuit does not work as a complete circuit — there is effectively an open circuit / the cells oppose each other and no usable current flows. Hence the lamp does not light up.

Question 37

4 marks

Suresh set up an experiment as shown below. He observed where the steel nail would move towards when both switches are closed.

(a) What was the aim of Suresh's experiment? (1m)
(b) When Suresh closed the switches for both set-ups X and Y, he observed that the steel nail moved towards one of the iron rods. Which set-up, X or Y, did the steel nail move towards? Explain why. (2m)
(c) Suresh would like to investigate whether the number of batteries would affect the result. Other than removing one battery from set-up X, suggest another change to set-up X. (1m)

Answer

(a) The aim was to find out whether the number of coils (turns) of wire around the fixed iron rod affects the magnetic strength of the electromagnet.
(b) The steel nail moved towards set-up Y. Set-up Y has more coils (turns) of wire around its fixed iron rod, so its electromagnet has a greater magnetic strength. The stronger electromagnet exerts a greater force of attraction on the steel nail, pulling it towards set-up Y.
(c) Add one more battery to set-up X (so that it has more batteries than before), while keeping everything else the same, to test the effect of the number of batteries. (Accept: add a battery to set-up X.)

Question 38

4 marks

Sue had three blocks, P, Q and R, that were of the same mass but of different materials. She pulled block P along the floor as shown in the diagram below and recorded the force needed to move block P.

She repeated the experiment using two other blocks, Q and R. The results for each block are recorded in the table below.

BlockForce needed to move the block (unit)
P18
Q10
R25
(a) What force must Sue overcome for the block to start moving? (1m)
(b) Based on the results given, which material P, Q or R is most suitable to make the sole of the shoes of a kitchen staff who needs to walk on a wet kitchen floor? Explain your answer. (2m)

Sue placed some straws under the block made of material Q as shown below.

(c) Sue noticed that the amount of force needed to pull the block was 5 units. Explain why she needed less force to pull the block. (1m)

Answer

(a) Sue must overcome frictional force for the block to start moving.
(b) Material R. The most force (25 units) is needed to move block R, which means material R has the greatest friction with the floor. When used to make the sole of the shoe, this gives the greatest frictional force / grip between the shoe and the wet floor, so the kitchen staff is least likely to slip.
(c) The straws act as rollers under the block. Rolling reduces the frictional force between the block and the floor, so less force is needed to pull the block.

Question 39

4 marks

Timothy placed a beaker of water on a metal plate. He heated up the metal plate with the flame from the stove as shown in the diagram below.

(a) Explain, in terms of heat transfer, how the beaker of water started to boil after five minutes. (1m)
(b) After switching off the stove, Timothy realised that the beaker of water started to cool down. Explain why. (1m)

Timothy prepared two sets of fish balls to be cooked in the beaker of boiling water. Set-up A consists of just one big fish ball with a mass of 500 g and set-up B consists of five fish balls with a total mass of 500 g as shown in the diagram below.

(c) Which set-up, A or B, would enable the fish ball(s) to be cooked faster? Explain why. (2m)

Answer

(a) The metal plate first gains heat from the flame of the stove. The heat is then transferred from the hot metal plate to the beaker and to the water (the cooler object), so the water gains heat. As the water keeps gaining heat, its temperature rises until it reaches its boiling point and boils.
(b) After the stove is switched off, the hot water is warmer than the surroundings, so it loses heat to the cooler surrounding air until its temperature falls to room temperature. This is why the water cools down.
(c) Set-up B. The five small fish balls have a larger total surface area exposed to the boiling water than the one big fish ball. With a greater surface area in contact with the hot water, the fish balls in set-up B gain heat from the boiling water faster, so they are cooked faster.

Question 40

3 marks

Yoana set up an experiment as shown in the diagram below. She released a toy car at the top of the ramp. The toy car would move down the ramp and hit the block, causing it to move.

Distance z represents the distance moved by the block after the toy car hit it, as shown in the diagram below.

(a) Based on the experiment set-up above, identify how distance z will be affected when each variable is changed. (1m)
variable changeddistance z
(i) decrease the mass of the blockincrease
(ii) decrease the angle of rampdecrease
(b) Explain, in terms of energy conversion, how increasing the mass of the toy car will affect distance z travelled by the block. (2m)

Answer

(a) (i) Decreasing the mass of the block → distance z increases (a lighter block is pushed further by the same hit). (ii) Decreasing the angle of the ramp → distance z decreases (the car arrives with less speed, so it pushes the block a shorter distance).
(b) When the mass of the toy car is increased, the car at the top of the ramp possesses greater gravitational potential energy. As it rolls down, this is converted into greater kinetic energy of the toy car. When the car hits the block, this larger kinetic energy is transferred to the block, giving the block more kinetic energy, so the block moves a greater distance z.
